optimist.name: Fiona Delgado-Chen
optimist.column:
The Giants lost yesterday, but I’m not here to talk about the scoreboard. I’m here to talk about the bullpen. You know the one—those guys in the pen who’ve been doing the heavy lifting since the first pitch of the season. They’re the unsung heroes, the ones who’ve kept this team afloat when the offense has sputtered. And let’s be honest, the offense has sputtered. But here’s the thing: the bullpen hasn’t blinked. They’ve thrown sliders with the precision of a coffee roaster’s latte art, and they’ve pitched through the chaos like they’ve got a secret plan.  

I’ve been saying this for months: the even-year magic is dormant but not dead. You can’t measure that kind of magic in wins and losses. You have to measure it in moments—like when a reliever comes in with a 3-2 count and shuts down a rally, or when a young hitter steps into the box with two strikes and does the unthinkable. That’s where the real work happens. The Giants aren’t just a team; they’re a lab. And in that lab, they’re testing theories, refining strategies, and proving that resilience is a muscle that gets stronger with every setback.  

Take Lindor, for example. He’s been the heartbeat of this team, isn’t he? A few weeks ago, he was the guy you’d bet on to hit a home run in the seventh inning. Now? He’s the guy you’d bet on to hit a double in the ninth. That’s not regression. That’s evolution. And Sandoval? He’s been the quiet storm in the middle of the lineup, grinding out hits and making the most of his at-bats. These are the kind of players who don’t need the spotlight. They just need the chance to prove themselves.  

I know the record stings. 6-9, 5.5 games back. But let’s not forget: this is early June. The calendar is still open,

pessimist.name: Bud Petrowski
pessimist.column:
The Giants lost again. Not that it’s a surprise, but the way they did it—another sloppy, error-prone performance that feels like a dress rehearsal for a playoff collapse—makes me want to throw my coffee in the trash. We signed who? To play where? Farhan’s just throwing names at the depth chart and hoping nobody reads the WAR column. I read it.  

Let’s start with the rotation. Jeff passably pitched, but he’s not the future. The rest of the starters? They’re either injured, underperforming, or both. Kyle Lewis is out, so we’re relying on a guy who’s been a liability since 2021. And don’t get me started on the bullpen. The closer’s been a joke for months, and the setup men? They’re either walking batters
